content:
Hello! I am Edward Isayev, a 3rd year PhD student in the Mathematics Department of Boston College. My research interests are in algebraic geometry, especially the enumerative geometry that arises from predictions in mathematical physics for certain Calabi–Yau hypersurfaces. I’m very fortunate to be advised by Qile Chen.
Before BC, I was an undergraduate master’s (MMath) student at the University of Edinburgh, from which I graduated with First-Class Honours in July 2024. I am originally from Ireland.
My CV is available in the top right-hand corner.
Feel free to contact me about anything either mathematical or otherwise at surname@bc.edu. I’m always happy to chat!

title: About Me

content:
This section serves as an archive for notes from various graduate-level topics courses I’ve taken over the years. In class, I use Goodnotes to write them up by hand, and I’ve recently embarked on a journey of Latexing them up. Progress will be slow, so if you need access to the raw handwritten pdfs, do reach out. Any errors made herein are entirely my own, and corrections would be greatly appreciated to my email.
Gromov-Witten Theory # Notes from a Spring 2026 course taught by Qile Chen. Topics include the fundamentals of \(\overline{\mathcal{M}}_{g,n}\), the string, dilaton and divisor equations, Frobenius manifolds, the Topological Recursion Relation, and Givental’s J-function. I will release them in chapters, and they should all be written up by the start of the next semester.

title: Course Notes

content:
Here you can find some expository notes on areas related to my thesis, which will eventually include toric geometry, orbifold cohomology, log geometry and more. They are deliberately informal and highly example-driven (frequently weighted projective space), written to complement the standard references. Any mistakes made herein are entirely my own, and corrections would be greatly appreciated to my email.
Equivariant Orbifold Cohomology (Forthcoming) # Notes on the practical ways of computing torus-equivariant Chen–Ruan cohomology for toric stacks, such as weighted projective space in the sense of Coates–Iritani–Jiang. In particular, they explore the combinatorial description of certain toric Deligne–Mumford stacks, and using this data, compute the full torus-equivariant cohomology ring. These concepts are worked out in full for \(\mathbb{P}(1,2,2)\).

title: Notes

content:
My research is centered on the Gromov–Witten theory of Calabi–Yau hypersurfaces, especially the structural predictions that arise from the BCOV conjectures.
In particular, I work with singular hypersurfaces in weighted projective space, and aim to establish their higher-genus structure by purely algebro-geometric means. I am especially interested in Yamaguchi–Yau polynomiality, the holomorphic anomaly equation and orbifold regularity.
For a gentle introduction to this area, the Expository Notes section of my website offers various short notes on the relevant prerequisites, such as orbifold cohomology and the \(I\)-function.
summary:

title: Research

content:
A list of seminar and reading-group talks I’ve given. The accompanying handwritten lecture notes are available upon request.
Counting is Hard: A Gentle Introduction to Enumerative Geometry # GIST Seminar · Spring 2026
Enumerative geometry is a beautiful area of mathematics, relating ideas from algebraic geometry, mathematical physics, symplectic topology and much more. In this talk, I will give a gentle introduction to the topic from the point of view of algebraic geometry, showcasing some of the key players involved, and explaining where the main difficulties arise. Despite being called “enumerative” geometry, very few numbers will appear, and I hope to explain why that’s okay.
Introduction to K-stability # Graduate Student Algebraic Geometry Seminar · Fall 2025
The introduction of K-stability in algebraic geometry has led to many major breakthroughs. After Yau proved the Calabi conjecture for canonically-polarised and, what are now called Calabi–Yau manifolds, the case for Fanos remained unsolved for over 40 years. There was some obstruction for Fano varieties admitting a certain nice class of metric. After decades of work, the obstruction was found to be K-stability. In this talk, we will focus on understanding what all this means, with no background in algebraic geometry assumed.
Homotopy Groups of Spheres # GIST Seminar · Spring 2025
Homotopy theory is a beautiful and rich area of mathematics. Part of what makes it so interesting is that one of its most central objects, the homotopy groups of spheres, are incredibly difficult to compute. By the indomitable human spirit, and the help of Serre and Freudenthal, we will compute infinitely many of them in this talk. Along the way, we will pick up some tools and motivation for studying stable homotopy theory.
TQFTs # GIST Seminar · Fall 2024
In what some have described as the greatest 65th birthday present of all time, Michael Atiyah gave to René Thom (and the world) the concept of the topological quantum field theory. This had led to an explosion of beautiful work in topology, algebraic geometry and, most recently, number theory. In this talk I hope to introduce just what these things are, classify them in low dimensions, and give some examples as to where they arise, and where they might be useful.
Introduction to Khovanov Homology # Skein Lasagna Modules Reading Group · Fall 2024
The Jones Polynomial has been lauded as one of the most important discoveries in knot theory. However, in practice, it can be tough to work with. In particular, it’s quite difficult to tell if it is a detector for any knots (this is currently known in exactly 0 cases; not even for the unknot!). At the very end of the previous millennium, Mikhail Khovanov introduced a categorification of it, which we now call Khovanov homology, that has proved both incredibly useful and beautiful. In this talk, we intend to explain just what is meant by categorification, (re-)introduce the Jones polynomial, and put these together in order to define Khovanov homology.
